sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 0g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a tuber, the question is whether the starch is behaving itself.
sections.subtitleText: 0g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a tuber, the question is whether the starch is behaving itself.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 17.4g of carbs. starch is doing most of the heavy lifting here. for tubers, the carb side decides whether the food feels stable or flimsy.

sections.narration: minerals are basically not part of the case here.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: pros first: easy comfort-food base with less baggage than junk food because that practical swap value matters. practical staple carb source because tubers are often judged by how usable they are day to day. whole-food carb package because that matters because it is a cleaner baseline than heavily refined carbs.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side: satiety payoff depends a lot on format because some versions are much steadier than others. protein support is weak because tubers still need help from the rest of the meal. preparation changes outcomes a lot because what you do to a tuber can make or break it.

closing.summary: Jerusalem Artichoke does have a few real-world upsides. but the nutrition case is still weak and the downsides drag it down hard. best only when you want easy carbs and can accept the missing upside.
closing.useCaseNote: Best only when you want easy carbs and can accept the missing upside.
closing.cta: Would you rank it the same, or nah?

explanation.summary: Fats are carrying this food most, while minerals are the biggest drag. It lands in D tier.
explanation.whyThisTier: This food lands in D tier with an overall score of 26 from a base nutrition score of 26. Context barely changed the result.
